narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 63% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for admission. This prediction is based on your profile: a Year 1 decile ranking of 4 (below the average of 5.71), a Year 3 decile of 3 (below the typical 5.53), an LSAT score of 33 (under the average 36.7), a strong undergraduate GPA of 3.90 (well above the 3.21 average), middle family income, male gender, White racial background, and full-time enrollment plans. While your undergrad performance stands out positively, the model focused on several key factors that tipped the scales toward predicting failure. The most influential was your Year 3 decile ranking, as your position in the lower third of your class pushed the prediction strongly toward failure compared to peers. Next in importance was your LSAT score, which being below average also contributed to that unfavorable direction. Your Year 1 decile followed closely, with its below-average standing adding further weight toward the failure prediction. Family income bracket, undergraduate GPA, racial background, full-time status, and gender rounded out the influences in that order, each nudging the outcome slightly more toward failure relative to typical students. Overall, your law school grade deciles and LSAT were the biggest drags, standing out as weaker than most applicants who get approved. In contrast, students who pass the bar on the first try usually have higher deciles across the board and stronger LSATs, even if other factors like yours align with the norm. To boost your future bar passage odds and reapply successfully, prioritize retaking the LSAT to aim above 36—many improve with targeted prep. Also, focus on elevating your law school performance early, perhaps through study groups or tutoring, since those deciles signal habits that correlate with bar success. Your solid undergrad GPA is a great foundation, so build on it with dedicated bar prep courses down the line. With these changes, you could shift your profile toward the successful majority. Keep pushing—you've already shown strength in undergrad, and that's a promising sign.
